title: NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel/competent-authority review.
evidence: Composite dashboard `UC-22.2.50`, Qualys export hash, `critical_suppliers.csv` revision ID, and weekly PDF archive row in `index=audit_evidence` referenced in the management body minutes.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(d) receives partial assurance because UC evidence proves the monitorable workflow or telemetry portion while legal approval and filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.21(2)(d) using NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search for NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring, dashboard panel, and archived result export in index=audit_evidence for Art.21(2)(d).

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(3) receives partial assurance because UC evidence proves the monitorable workflow or telemetry portion while legal approval and filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.21(3) using NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search for NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring, dashboard panel, and archived result export in index=audit_evidence for Art.21(3).

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.22 receives partial assurance because UC evidence proves the monitorable workflow or telemetry portion while legal approval and filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.22 using NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search for NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring, dashboard panel, and archived result export in index=audit_evidence for Art.22.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Seed Qualys events for two tier1 suppliers where open QID counts exceed the threshold and CVE severity maps to the high bucket; the saved search lists both supplier_name values with distinct asset counts and the auditor can open the drilldown to raw events.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Replay the same QID payloads after updating `critical_suppliers.csv` to mark those hosts as divested (`supplier_tier=exited`) so the lookup drops the rows and the saved search returns an empty statistic with a log entry confirming the suppressing lookup version.

dataSources: `index=vuln` `sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection` (HOSTNAME, QID, CVE_ID, Severity, STATUS) via Splunk Add-on for Qualys ([Splunkbase 2964](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2964)); `lookup=critical_suppliers.csv` (managed_asset, supplier_name, supplier_tier, jump_host); `index=sbom` `sourcetype=sbom:cyclonedx` or `sourcetype=spdx:json` with `component.name`, `component.version`; `index=pam` `sourcetype=cyberark:session` (user, target_host, duration_min) via Splunk Add-on for CyberArk ([Splunkbase 2891](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2891)).
app: Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964), Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (Splunkbase 2891), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928); optional Splunk SOAR (Splunkbase 5165) for supplier playbooks.
spl:
index=vuln sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection earliest=-30d NOT (STATUS=Fixed OR lower(STATUS)="fixed")
| eval asset=lower(trim(coalesce(HOSTNAME, host, fqdn)))
| lookup critical_suppliers.csv managed_asset AS asset OUTPUT supplier_name supplier_tier jump_host
| where supplier_tier IN ("critical","tier1")
| eval severity_score=case(Severity="5",5,Severity="4",4,match(lower(coalesce(severity,"")),"critical"),5,match(lower(coalesce(severity,"")),"high"),4,0)
| where severity_score>=4
| stats dc(QID) as open_findings dc(CVE_ID) as distinct_cves dc(asset) as impacted_assets by supplier_name supplier_tier
| where open_findings>5 OR impacted_assets>8
| sort - open_findings
| table supplier_name supplier_tier open_findings distinct_cves impacted_assets
description: Correlates Qualys host findings that are still open and high severity against a `critical_suppliers.csv` inventory so only tier-one vendor estates with concentrated plugin counts appear, preparing companion SBOM and CyberArk panels.
value: Board packs gain a single narrative—"which indispensable supplier has the most serious unfixed exposures right now"—without three separate meetings with procurement, SecOps, and engineering, and it plugs directly into Art.21(3)/22 governance discussions about dependency management and incident cooperation.
implementation: (1) Ingest Qualys host detections into `index=vuln` with TA 2964 modular inputs hitting Qualys API **/api/3.0/fo/asset/host/vm/detection**. (2) Maintain `critical_suppliers.csv` under change control; columns must include managed_asset (FQDN lower-case) and supplier_tier. (3) Schedule this search daily; clone a second search for `index=sbom` CycloneDX joins on `component.version`. (4) Add CyberArk panel: `index=pam` stats on `target_host` where `jump_host` matches lookup. (5) Export composite PDF weekly to `index=audit_evidence`. (6) Optional: open ES (263) notable event when `open_findings>25`.
visualization: Supplier risk table, SBOM component treemap (optional panel), CyberArk vendor duration heatmap, ES risk index line chart.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Alerts where nodename=Alerts.Alerts by Alerts.signature | head 20
dataModelAcceleration: Recommended where ES Alerts datamodel is accelerated for executive rollups; otherwise rely on raw `index=vuln` searches scoped to 30 days.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Qualys STATUS field inconsistent after scanner upgrade** — Distinguish: uppercase `FIXED` vs `Fixed`. Suppress: normalize with `eval st=lower(STATUS)` then `where st!="fixed"`.
• **Shared scanning appliance shows duplicate QIDs** — Distinguish: same CVE listed per interface. Suppress: `dedup QID asset` before `stats`.
• **SBOM duplicates dev artifacts** — Distinguish: test images inflate component counts. Suppress: lookup `sbom_scope_exceptions.csv` marking `environment=prod_only`.
• **CyberArk sessions during approved vendor cutover** — Distinguish: CHG record exists in ServiceNow. Suppress: time-bound exception register `supplier_access_window.csv`.
• **critical_suppliers.csv stale after divestiture** — Distinguish: assets still flagged tier1 though business sold division. Suppress: nightly sync from ServiceNow CMDB via Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow REST input filtered with `u_active=true`.
equipmentModels: CyberArk Privileged Access Security (PAM session logs for vendor jump-host monitoring)
Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964, host detection vulnerability data)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, supplier contract and assessment data)
Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud (HEC for SBOM ingestion from CI/CD pipelines)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, Assets & Identities for supplier tagging)
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• This UC implements NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) — supply chain security, and Art.22 — coordinated security risk assessments of critical supply chains. It provides a composite view of third-party risk across three dimensions: (1) vulnerability exposure on supplier-managed assets (Qualys detections on infrastructure operated by vendors), (2) software supply chain risk through SBOM analysis (known CVEs in vendor-supplied software components), and (3) vendor access patterns via privileged access management logs (CyberArk sessions through supplier jump-hosts). NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) specifically requires entities to address "supply chain security, including security-related aspects concerning the relationships between each entity and its direct suppliers or service providers." This UC operationalises that requirement.
• **Delineation from UC-22.2.16 and UC-22.2.25**: UC-22.2.16 monitors network traffic volume and assessment overdue dates for suppliers (quantitative — bytes in/out, assessment cadence). UC-22.2.25 tracks assessment coverage and Dependabot alerts (breadth — are all suppliers assessed?). This UC (22.2.50) goes deeper: it examines specific vulnerability findings on supplier assets, analyses SBOM component exposure, and correlates with real-time vendor access sessions. Together, the three UCs provide defence-in-depth for supply chain monitoring.
• **Splunk Add-on for Qualys** (Splunkbase 2964): configured to pull host detection data via the Qualys API. The modular input requires a Qualys API user with "Reader" permissions (least privilege — no scan launch capability). Credentials stored in Splunk's credential store (`passwords.conf`). Events land in `index=vuln sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection` with fields: `HOSTNAME` (the scanned asset), `QID` (Qualys vulnerability identifier), `SEVERITY` (1-5 scale), `STATUS` (Active, New, Re-Opened, Fixed), `FIRST_FOUND_DATETIME`, `LAST_FOUND_DATETIME`, `CVE_ID_LIST` (associated CVE identifiers). Network requirement: outbound HTTPS (443/tcp) from the Heavy Forwarder to the Qualys API endpoint.
• **Critical suppliers lookup**: `critical_suppliers.csv` with columns: `managed_asset` (hostname or FQDN of an asset managed by the supplier — the join key to Qualys data), `supplier_name` (e.g., "Acme Cloud Services", "TechVendor AS"), `supplier_tier` (critical, tier1, tier2 — NIS2 focuses on critical suppliers), `jump_host` (the CyberArk jump-host hostname used by this vendor's personnel for remote access — the join key to PAM data), `contract_id` (reference to the ServiceNow contract), `sbom_repo` (the Git repository or artifact registry where the vendor's SBOM is stored). This CSV is maintained jointly by Procurement and the CISO office. It must be approved by legal before deployment — it may contain commercially sensitive information about vendor relationships.
• **SBOM data**: Software Bill of Materials in CycloneDX or SPDX format, ingested via HEC or scripted input to `index=sbom sourcetype=sbom:cyclonedx` or `sourcetype=sbom:spdx`. Each SBOM event contains: `component_name` (e.g., "log4j-core"), `component_version` (e.g., "2.14.1"), `supplier_name` (the vendor who supplied the software), `cve_id` (known CVE affecting this version — enriched by the SBOM tooling, e.g., Syft, Grype, or OWASP Dependency-Track). SBOMs are typically generated during the CI/CD pipeline and POSTed to HEC on each build.
• **CyberArk PAM logs**: CyberArk Privileged Access Security session logs ingested to `index=pam sourcetype=cyberark:session`. Key fields: `user` (the vendor's personnel username), `target_host` (the system being accessed), `session_start`, `session_end`, `session_duration_sec`, `reason` (the stated justification for access), `policy_name` (the CyberArk policy governing this access). The SPL joins PAM sessions to the `jump_host` field in `critical_suppliers.csv` to identify vendor access events.
• **RBAC**: role `nis2_supply_chain` with `srchIndexesAllowed = vuln, sbom, pam, audit_evidence`. SBOM data may reveal the technology stack of vendor-supplied software (commercially sensitive). PAM logs reveal who accessed what and when (operationally sensitive). Restrict access to procurement, CISO office, and designated supply chain risk analysts.
• **Index retention**: `index=vuln` ≥ 1 year (vulnerability trend analysis). `index=sbom` ≥ 2 years (SBOM version history). `index=pam` ≥ 1 year (access audit trail). `index=audit_evidence` ≥ 7 years.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**Qualys host detections:**
Install the Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964) on a Heavy Forwarder. Configure the modular input:
```ini
[qualys://host_detection]
api_url = https://qualysapi.qg2.apps.qualys.eu
api_username = svc-splunk-qualys
interval = 3600
index = vuln
sourcetype = qualys:hostdetection
detection_params = status=Active,New,Re-Opened&severities=3,4,5
```
The `detection_params` filter ensures only active high/critical findings are pulled. Lower-severity findings (1-2) can be included for completeness but significantly increase volume.

Verify:
```
index=vuln sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection earliest=-7d
| stats count dc(HOSTNAME) as scanned_hosts dc(QID) as unique_vulns values(SEVERITY) as severities
```
Expected: `scanned_hosts` should match the Qualys dashboard's "Assets Scanned" count. `severities` should include 3, 4, 5.

**SBOM ingestion (CycloneDX via HEC):**
In the CI/CD pipeline, after SBOM generation (e.g., `syft packages . -o cyclonedx-json`), POST to HEC:
```bash
curl -k https://hec.splunk.example.com:8088/services/collector/event \
  -H "Authorization: Splunk <token>" \
  -d '{"event": <sbom_json>, "sourcetype": "sbom:cyclonedx", "index": "sbom"}'
```
For vulnerability correlation, use Grype or OWASP Dependency-Track to enrich the SBOM with CVE data before ingestion. The enriched SBOM should contain `vulnerabilities[].id` (CVE ID) and `vulnerabilities[].ratings[].severity` for each affected component.

Verify:
```
index=sbom sourcetype=sbom:cyclonedx earliest=-30d
| stats count dc(component_name) as components dc(cve_id) as known_cves dc(supplier_name) as suppliers
```

**CyberArk PAM session logs:**
CyberArk exports session logs via syslog or the CyberArk Privileged Threat Analytics (PTA) API. Configure a syslog input on the Heavy Forwarder or a scripted input polling the PTA REST API:
```ini
[monitor:///var/log/cyberark/session_audit.log]
sourcetype = cyberark:session
index = pam
```
Verify:
```
index=pam sourcetype=cyberark:session earliest=-7d
| stats count dc(user) as vendor_users dc(target_host) as targets
```

**Critical suppliers lookup:**
Place `critical_suppliers.csv` in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-supply/lookups/`. Define in `transforms.conf`:
```ini
[critical_suppliers]
filename = critical_suppliers.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
```
Validate: `| inputlookup critical_suppliers.csv | stats count dc(supplier_name) as suppliers dc(managed_asset) as assets`. Expected: `suppliers` matches the number of critical/tier1 vendors in your procurement register.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.50 NIS2 Critical Supplier Risk Evidence`.

Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

• **Three-branch architecture**: the search produces findings from three independent data sources, merged into a uniform output:
  - **Branch 1 (Qualys detections)**: filters to active/new/re-opened vulnerabilities of SEVERITY ≥ 4 on assets managed by critical/tier1 suppliers. Each finding represents an unpatched vulnerability on a supplier-managed asset — a direct NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) risk signal.
  - **Branch 2 (SBOM CVE exposure)**: identifies vendor-supplied software components with known critical/high CVEs. Each finding represents a software supply chain risk — the vendor's code contains a known vulnerability that could affect the entity.
  - **Branch 3 (PAM anomalies)**: detects unusual vendor access patterns through CyberArk jump-hosts. `session_duration_sec > 28800` (8 hours) or sessions outside business hours (before 07:00 or after 19:00 local time) are flagged. These may indicate legitimate maintenance or unauthorised activity.

• **`lookup critical_suppliers.csv managed_asset AS asset OUTPUT supplier_name supplier_tier jump_host`**: enriches each Qualys finding with the supplier context. Only assets that appear in the critical suppliers CSV produce results — vulnerabilities on non-supplier assets are handled by other UCs.

• **`where supplier_tier IN ("critical","tier1")`**: focuses on the most important suppliers. NIS2 Art.22 specifically concerns "critical supply chains" — tier2 suppliers can be monitored with lower priority.

• **SBOM CVE severity filtering**: `where severity IN ("critical","high")` focuses on exploitable vulnerabilities. Medium/low findings are voluminous and typically managed through routine patching cycles rather than supply chain risk escalation.

• **PAM session anomaly thresholds**: the 8-hour and out-of-hours thresholds are starting points. Tune based on your vendor access patterns: some managed service providers have 24/7 NOCs (out-of-hours access is normal), while others only perform scheduled maintenance (any out-of-hours access is suspicious).

Schedule: cron `0 8 * * 1,4` (Monday and Thursday at 08:00). Time range: embedded in the SPL (30d for Qualys and SBOM, 7d for PAM). Trigger: `Number of results > 0`.

Alert actions: (1) For SEVERITY 5 Qualys findings on critical supplier assets: PagerDuty page to the CISO with supplier name. (2) For SBOM critical CVEs: email to procurement and the vendor relationship manager. (3) For PAM anomalies: email to the SOC for investigation. (4) Write all results to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:supplier_risk:evidence`.

Extended search — supplier risk scorecard:
```spl
| inputlookup critical_suppliers.csv where supplier_tier IN ("critical","tier1")
| stats dc(managed_asset) as managed_assets by supplier_name supplier_tier
| join type=left supplier_name
    [search index=vuln sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection SEVERITY>=4 earliest=-30d NOT STATUS=Fixed
     | lookup critical_suppliers.csv managed_asset AS asset OUTPUT supplier_name
     | where isnotnull(supplier_name)
     | stats dc(QID) as open_vulns sum(eval(if(SEVERITY=5,1,0))) as crit_vulns by supplier_name]
| join type=left supplier_name
    [search index=sbom sourcetype=sbom:cyclonedx severity IN ("critical","high") earliest=-30d
     | stats dc(cve_id) as sbom_cves by supplier_name]
| join type=left supplier_name
    [search index=pam sourcetype=cyberark:session earliest=-7d
     | lookup critical_suppliers.csv jump_host AS target_host OUTPUT supplier_name
     | where isnotnull(supplier_name)
     | stats count as pam_sessions avg(session_duration_sec) as avg_session_sec by supplier_name]
| fillnull open_vulns value=0 crit_vulns value=0 sbom_cves value=0 pam_sessions value=0
| eval risk_score=crit_vulns*10 + open_vulns + sbom_cves*5
| table supplier_name supplier_tier managed_assets open_vulns crit_vulns sbom_cves pam_sessions avg_session_sec risk_score
| sort - risk_score
```
This scorecard aggregates all three risk dimensions per supplier into a single `risk_score`. Present quarterly to the board.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Qualys parity**: for a known critical supplier asset, compare the Qualys Cloud Platform UI (Vulnerabilities > Host Detections > filter by hostname) with the Splunk results. The number of active SEVERITY ≥ 4 findings should match.

(b) **SBOM CVE detection**: generate a test SBOM for a project with a known critical CVE (e.g., log4j-core 2.14.1). Ingest to Splunk. Run the search. Confirm the CVE appears with the correct `supplier_name` and `component_version`.

(c) **PAM anomaly detection**: have a test user establish a CyberArk session through a supplier jump-host outside business hours. Verify the session appears in the PAM branch results.

(d) **Supplier enrichment**: run: `index=vuln sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection SEVERITY>=4 earliest=-7d | lookup critical_suppliers.csv managed_asset AS asset OUTPUT supplier_name | stats count by supplier_name`. Verify that `supplier_name` is populated for known supplier assets and null for non-supplier assets.

(e) **Risk scorecard validation**: run the extended search. Verify that suppliers with more critical vulnerabilities have higher `risk_score`. Cross-reference with the procurement team's vendor risk assessment to confirm alignment.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Critical Supplier Risk`):
• **Row 1 — Risk headline**: Single-value `Critical suppliers monitored`. Single-value `Suppliers with open critical vulns` (red ≥ 1). Single-value `SBOM CVEs (30d)`. Single-value `Unusual vendor sessions (7d)`.
• **Row 2 — Supplier risk scorecard**: table from the extended search with `risk_score` colour gradient (green ≤ 10, amber 11-50, red > 50).
• **Row 3 — Vulnerability heatmap**: `index=vuln sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection SEVERITY>=4 earliest=-90d | lookup critical_suppliers.csv managed_asset AS asset OUTPUT supplier_name | where isnotnull(supplier_name) | timechart span=1w dc(QID) by supplier_name`. Shows vulnerability trends per supplier.
• **Row 4 — Vendor access timeline**: `index=pam sourcetype=cyberark:session earliest=-30d | lookup critical_suppliers.csv jump_host AS target_host OUTPUT supplier_name | where isnotnull(supplier_name) | timechart span=1d count by supplier_name`. Shows access frequency per vendor over the last month.

Runbook (owner: Procurement / CISO):
1. Twice-weekly: review alert findings. For each supplier with critical vulnerabilities:
   - Contact the vendor's security liaison. Request a remediation timeline. If the vendor cannot remediate within the contractual SLA, escalate to procurement for contract review.
   - For SBOM CVEs: determine if the affected component is in the critical path. If yes, request a vendor patch or mitigation. If no, document the risk acceptance.
   - For PAM anomalies: correlate with the vendor's change calendar. If the session was not scheduled, request an explanation from the vendor.
2. Monthly: review the supplier risk scorecard. Present the top-5 highest-risk suppliers to the CISO. Identify trends: are suppliers improving or deteriorating?
3. Quarterly: present to the board as part of the NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) evidence package. Include: supplier risk scores, remediation timelines, and any contract actions taken.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Qualys API returns HTTP 409 (Concurrent Sessions).** The Qualys API limits concurrent sessions per user. If the Splunk TA and a manual Qualys scan are running simultaneously, one will fail. Fix: use a dedicated Qualys API user for Splunk with no manual access. Or increase the polling interval to reduce concurrency.

• **`managed_asset` doesn't match Qualys `HOSTNAME`.** The lookup uses `managed_asset` as the join key against `HOSTNAME` from Qualys. If the CMDB uses FQDNs ("srv-vendor-01.internal.example.com") but Qualys uses short names ("srv-vendor-01"), the join fails. Fix: normalise in the SPL: `eval asset=lower(mvindex(split(HOSTNAME,"."),0))` before the lookup. Or add both FQDN and short name to the CSV.

• **SBOM ingestion produces duplicate components.** Multiple builds of the same software produce multiple SBOM events with the same `component_name` and `component_version`. The `dc(cve_id)` handles this correctly (deduplicates CVEs), but `count` may be inflated. Fix: for the scorecard, use `dc(cve_id)` rather than `count` for the SBOM branch.

• **CyberArk jump-host naming convention mismatch.** The `jump_host` in the CSV doesn't match the `target_host` in CyberArk logs. CyberArk may log the target as an IP address while the CSV uses a hostname. Fix: add both hostname and IP to the CSV (duplicate rows), or resolve hostnames to IPs in the SPL: `| lookup dns_lookup ip AS target_host OUTPUT hostname | eval target_host=coalesce(hostname, target_host)`.

• **Supplier with no managed assets in Qualys.** A critical supplier manages cloud services (SaaS) rather than on-premise infrastructure. Their assets are not scanned by the organisation's Qualys subscription. Fix: the Qualys branch will correctly produce no results for this supplier. Use the SBOM branch instead — the vendor's software components should be in the SBOM. If neither data source covers the supplier, add a `monitoring_gap=1` flag to the CSV and display it on the dashboard.

• **PAM sessions from vendor automated tools.** Some vendors use automated scripts through CyberArk for scheduled maintenance (e.g., nightly batch jobs). These sessions may be very long (24h+) and trigger the anomaly threshold every run. Fix: add the vendor's service account to an exception register: `| lookup pam_service_accounts.csv user OUTPUT is_service_account | where is_service_account!="yes"`. Or adjust the threshold for specific jump-hosts in the suppliers CSV.
grandmaExplanation: We compare the list of suppliers you cannot afford to lose with the latest serious security holes and the time outside technicians spend inside your systems, so leaders see one clear picture before they sign off on risk.
